unitTitle: KS1 Y1 Programming animations (Teach Computing — adapted)

lessons.title: Comparing tools — moving a sprite in ScratchJr
lessons.objectives:
I can find the commands to move a sprite
I can use commands to move a sprite
I can compare different programming tools
lessons.outline:
Routine first (same every lesson). Vocabulary on board: ScratchJr · sprite · command · programming area · compare. TA: ScratchJr open on every device; everyone has the starter worksheet. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud. Recap: last unit we used Bee-Bot floor robots; this unit we use ScratchJr on screen.
1. (0–10) Starter — meet ScratchJr. Show the ScratchJr screen. Pupils LABEL the screen on the starter worksheet (drag Cat sprite, Blocks, Green flag onto the picture).
   • Support: choose which one is the sprite.  • Core: name the blue blocks (commands).  • Challenge: where do the blocks go (programming area).
2. (10–20) I-do → we-do — move the cat. Teacher drags a blue block into the programming area and TAPS it. Likely error: drags the block but forgets to tap it → fix-words “drag it, then tap it.”
3. (20–40) You-do — make the cat move. Pupils try move right, left, up, down. On the activity worksheet they LABEL the four move blocks with their direction. TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
   • Support: tick which block goes right.  • Core: pick the block to go up.  • Challenge: compare ScratchJr and a Bee-Bot (one same, one different).
4. (40–50) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”; each pupil shows the cat moving and gives one compare idea.
Differentiation on the SAME task, low floor / high ceiling — nobody’s screen looks “less done”. No flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Joining blocks — Start, End and run
lessons.objectives:
I can use more than one block by joining them together
I can use a Start block in a program
I can run my program
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: block · join · Start block · End block · run · program · background · algorithm · predict. TA: ScratchJr open; starter worksheet out. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud. Recap: last lesson we moved the cat with one command.
1. (0–5) Add a background. Teacher models adding the Classroom background; pupils add it.
2. (5–12) Starter — order the steps. On the starter worksheet pupils ORDER the steps to build and run a program (Start block → join move blocks → End block → tap the green flag).
   • Support: which block goes first (Start).  • Core: what the green flag does.  • Challenge: why we join blocks with no gaps.
3. (12–25) I-do → we-do — join the blocks. Yellow Start first, blue move blocks in the middle, red End last; they click like a jigsaw, read left to right. Likely error: a gap between blocks so only the first runs → fix-words “push them together until they click.”
4. (25–45) You-do — build and run. Pupils make a program (Start + five move blocks + End) and run it with the green flag in full-screen. They PREDICT the path on the activity worksheet using the joined-program image, then run to check.
   • Support: which block starts it.  • Core: count the move-right blocks.  • Challenge: what changes if blocks are swapped.
5. (45–50) Plenary — self-tick the 3 “I can…”; each pupil shows a joined program that runs.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Make a change — values in a block
lessons.objectives:
I can find blocks that have numbers
I can change the value
I can say what happens when I change a value
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: value · change · effect · block. TA: ScratchJr open; starter worksheet out. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud. Recap: last lesson we joined blocks and ran a program.
1. (0–5) Set the problem — long programs. Show that using the same block over and over makes very long programs.
2. (5–20) Starter — hunt the blocks. Pupils hunt each block colour to find blocks with a number underneath (the value). On the starter worksheet they LABEL the value and the number pad on the block image.
   • Support: where is the value (the number underneath).  • Core: how to change it (tap number, use the pad).  • Challenge: why change a number instead of adding blocks.
3. (20–35) I-do → we-do — one block, change the value. Instead of five move-right blocks, use one with the value 5. Same result, fewer blocks.
4. (35–48) You-do — change a value. Pupils change a value and watch the effect (5 = far, 2 = shorter). Predict-then-run on the activity worksheet, then a 1–5 confidence scale. Likely error: adds more blocks instead of changing the number → fix-words “don’t add a block, change the number.” TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
   • Support: which value moves furthest.  • Core: say what happens 1→5.  • Challenge: why one block with value 5 beats five blocks.
5. (48–50) Plenary — spot-the-difference between two programs; self-tick the 3 “I can…”.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

lessons.title: Adding sprites — each sprite has its own instructions
lessons.objectives:
I can show that a project can include more than one sprite
I can delete a sprite
I can add blocks to each of my sprites
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: sprite · instructions · delete · program · algorithm. TA: ScratchJr open; starter worksheet out. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud. Recap: last lesson we changed a value.
1. (0–10) Starter — what is wrong? Show the cat on the moon. Pupils discuss the mismatch and choose a sprite that fits (an astronaut) on the starter worksheet.
   • Support: what is the problem.  • Core: which sprite fits the moon.  • Challenge: why a sprite should match the background.
2. (10–20) More than one sprite. Show the underwater project with several sea-animal sprites. Pupils count them and SORT sprites to the right background (underwater/moon/arctic) on the activity worksheet.
3. (20–30) I-do → we-do — each sprite has its own program. Tap a sprite to select it; its programming area shows its own blocks. Likely error: adds blocks while the wrong sprite is selected → fix-words “tap the sprite first, then add its blocks.”
4. (30–45) You-do — add and delete sprites. Pupils open the sample project, delete the old programs, and add blocks to each sprite. They tick which facts about sprites are true (multi-select). TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
   • Support: can you see more than one sprite (yes/no).  • Core: count the sprites.  • Challenge: multi-select the true facts about sprites.
5. (45–50) Plenary — match each sprite to its program; self-tick the 3 “I can…”.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: A cat sprite on the moon (Teach Computing).png

lessons.title: Project design — planning a Space race
lessons.objectives:
I can choose appropriate artwork for my project
I can decide how each sprite will move
I can create an algorithm for each sprite
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: sprite · background · appropriate · algorithm. TA: starter worksheet + colouring pencils ready; keep every sheet for next lesson. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud. Today is DESIGN only — no ScratchJr; we build it next lesson.
1. (0–8) Starter — meet the project. Show the three rockets Space race. Pupils recall what an algorithm is (a precise set of instructions in order) on the starter worksheet.
   • Support: what is an algorithm.  • Core: when do we build it (next lesson, after design).  • Challenge: why plan before coding.
2. (8–20) Choose your artwork. Pupils sketch a background and colour their three rockets (first, second, third).
3. (20–30) Decide how they move. Pupils decide each rocket’s path (up to the sky / across to a planet).
4. (30–45) You-do — write the algorithm. Example on the board: Start → Speed fast → Move up 6 → Stop. On the activity worksheet pupils ORDER that algorithm, then write their own. Likely error: an algorithm ScratchJr can’t do → fix-words “only use moves you can find in ScratchJr.” TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
   • Support: order the given algorithm.  • Core: write the directions their rocket moves.  • Challenge: write their own algorithm, Start to Stop.
5. (45–50) Plenary — match a finished project to its design; self-tick the 3 “I can…”. Keep all sheets for Lesson 6.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.

resources.title: Three rockets — a Space race (Teach Computing).jpg
resources.sourceAttribution: Teach Computing Curriculum © Raspberry Pi Foundation, OGL v3.0.

lessons.title: Following my design — build and test the Space race
lessons.objectives:
I can use sprites that match my design
I can add programming blocks based on my algorithm
I can test the programs I have created
lessons.outline:
Routine first. Vocabulary on board: sprite · design · programming blocks · algorithm · test. TA: hand back each pupil’s design and algorithm from last lesson; ScratchJr open; testing worksheet ready. Read the 3 “I can…” aloud. Recap: last lesson we designed the Space race.
1. (0–8) Starter — use your design. Show the Space race. Pupils recall they will build it from their design and algorithm, and what testing means, on the starter worksheet.
   • Support: what helps you build today (design + algorithm).  • Core: what does test mean.  • Challenge: what to change if the rocket falls short.
2. (8–18) Build your artwork. Pupils choose the background and design the three rockets to match their plan; undo/redo helps.
3. (18–30) I-do → we-do — add the blocks. Read the algorithm, add blocks to the matching rocket. Likely error: blocks added to the wrong rocket → fix-words “tap the right rocket first, then add its blocks.”
4. (30–45) You-do — build and test. On the activity worksheet pupils ORDER the build-and-test steps, run each program, and record whether it matched the plan (yes/no) plus a 1–5 ‘how well it matched’ scale. TA: prompt, do not do it for them.
   • Support: did it work (yes/no).  • Core: what one thing they changed.  • Challenge: why testing matters.
5. (45–50) Plenary — share projects with a partner/teacher; self-tick the 3 “I can…”. Recap the journey: design → algorithm → code → test.
Differentiation on the SAME task; no flashing/sound; movement breaks are routine.
